summary: Mitchell Hashimoto recounts his naive approach to business banking as a 22-year-old startup founder, from opening a Chase account with $20,000 to managing $35 million four years later. He repeatedly ignored his banker Alex's calls, not understanding the significance of their relationship, then abruptly transferred all funds to Silicon Valley Bank without warning. Years later he discovered he had become a cautionary tale at Chase branches across Los Angeles, learned that Alex had faced consequences, and had to navigate a surreal process to close the forgotten account and withdraw $1M via cashier's check. The story culminates in his recognition that banks aren't fungible and relationships matter, even if no lasting harm came from his choices.
key_points: Banks are not interchangeable institutions - local branches and individual bankers have their own performance metrics, relationships, and stakes in your account
A startup account growing from $20K to $35M made the original banker a rising star, and its sudden departure had real professional consequences
Institutional knowledge spreads: HashiCorp became a case study for Chase branches on how startup financial patterns differ from typical businesses
Neglected accounts create problems: the forgotten Chase account accumulated over $100K in fraud before anyone noticed
Sometimes reasonable decisions made in good faith with limited knowledge still lead to cringe-worthy situations in hindsight
money_quotes: I figured: you put money in, you take money out, they're all the same.
Some wheels have started turning in Southern California, and I just hadn't known it yet.
I switched banks, that's all there is to it. Thanks and goodbye. I never talk to Alex ever again.
The teller looks at the paper, then looks at me, then looks back at the paper, then asks 'Are you the HashiCorp guy?'
And then, overnight, the account went to $0. Without talking to anyone, without any prior warning, that account was gone.
I literally asked: 'like, green paper money cash?' He says yes. The balance in the account is somewhere around $1M.
He literally writes down a phone number on a piece of paper. This is all feeling so surreal.
I want to make it clear that Chase could've been an excellent banking partner. I never gave them the chance.
key_insight: Business banking relationships are not transactional utilities but human relationships with real stakes, and treating them otherwise can have unintended consequences for everyone involved.
